surface.name: Tutorial: Make your first Git commit
surface.selection_basis: Workshop selection policy (2026-07-19): when official docs present peer routes, commit to the fastest and most commonly used documented route. Prefer the vendor's first-listed or recommended quickstart, dashboard/console over CLI when both are peers, and the route with an explicit vendor time claim or fewest documented steps when tied. Committed to reconstructed surface `Unresolved platform-wide, with Tutorial: Make your first Git commit preserved as the representative core-code journey`. Prior: GitLab's getting-started index lists many peer workflows and does not designate one platform-wide first success. The first-commit tutorial is preserved in full because it starts from account, Git, and SSH prerequisites and ends with a visible README change and commit in GitLab. Choosing it instead of first project, first issue, first pipeline, first API call, first runner, or first deployment requires human judgment.

alternatives_considered.name: Create and run your first GitLab CI/CD pipeline
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: This is an explicitly named first-success route, but it assumes an existing project and Maintainer or Owner role and measures CI/CD rather than core repository use.

alternatives_considered.name: Create a project
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: Project creation is an earlier possible boundary but may be too shallow compared with an actual code change.

alternatives_considered.name: Get started extending GitLab
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: API and integration work is a peer developer intent with separate credentials and success.

alternatives_considered.name: Create, register, and run your own project runner
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: This route adds local Runner installation and registration and ends at a runner-executed job.

entry_point.developer_intent: Choose one GitLab workflow and reach its first observable result; the representative path creates, edits, commits, pushes, merges, and verifies a repository change.
assumed_prior_state: No GitLab.com or organization-provided account, project, local Git installation, SSH key, runner, or product workflow is assumed.
No decision has been supplied about GitLab.com, Self-Managed, or Dedicated.

documented_first_success.official_milestone: In the selected first-Git-commit tutorial, first success is demonstrated when the project README shows Hello world! I'm using Git! and the Last commit column shows I added text to the README file.
documented_first_success.normalized_outcome: Create a GitLab project, clone it over SSH, edit README.md on a branch, commit and push the change, merge it into the default branch, push, and verify the content and commit in GitLab.
documented_first_success.observable_completion_signal: The project README displays Hello world! I'm using Git! and the Last commit column displays I added text to the README file.

boundary_evidence.official_label_or_terminal_state: README.md shows Hello world! I'm using Git! and Last commit shows I added text to the README file.

documented_first_success.why_this_is_the_boundary: Committed under workshop fastest/most-used policy; terminal taken from the reconstructed official path.

prerequisites.requirement: A GitLab.com, GitLab Self-Managed, or GitLab Dedicated user account that can sign in.
Permission to create a project in the chosen namespace and push or merge to its default branch.
Git installed on the local machine.
A Git author name and email usable by git commit.
Network access to the selected GitLab instance over HTTPS and SSH.
A declared GitLab workflow and completion signal.

primary_path.action: Open GitLab's getting-started index and choose the developer workflow to measure.
details: The index offers separate routes for Git, projects, planning, code, CI/CD, Runner, security, deployment, infrastructure, monitoring, GitLab Duo, and extending GitLab.
primary_path.success_signal: The developer names one intended first result.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: The index does not select a universal path.

primary_path.action: Obtain access to a GitLab instance and sign in.
details: The first-commit tutorial says to ensure you can sign in and to create a GitLab.com account if the organization does not have GitLab.
primary_path.input: GitLab.com account, organization-provided account, or self-managed registration.
primary_path.success_signal: The GitLab interface is accessible.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: GitLab.com signup details are not enumerated in the tutorial; self-managed registration can be disabled or require administrator action.

primary_path.action: Install Git on the local machine.
details: Git is an explicit before-you-begin requirement.
primary_path.input: Supported local operating system and package method.
primary_path.success_signal: A Git command can run locally.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: Installation steps depend on operating system and are linked out of the tutorial.

primary_path.action: Ensure OpenSSH client version 6.5 or later is installed.
details: OpenSSH is pre-installed on GNU/Linux, macOS, and Windows 10 according to the SSH guide.
Run ssh -V to inspect the version.
primary_path.success_signal: ssh -V reports a compatible version.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: Older SSH versions use insecure MD5 signatures and do not satisfy the prerequisite.

primary_path.action: Check whether an SSH key pair already exists.
details: Inspect the .ssh subdirectory for a public and private key pair in one of the documented formats.
ED25519 is preferred; RSA should be at least 4096 bits.
primary_path.output: Existing suitable key or determination that a key must be created.
primary_path.success_signal: A supported public/private key pair is identified.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: If no pair exists, generate one.

primary_path.action: If needed, generate an SSH key pair.
details: Run ssh-keygen -t ed25519 -C "<comment>" or the documented RSA alternative.
Accept or choose a filename and directory, choose and confirm a passphrase, and observe the generated-key confirmation.
primary_path.input: OpenSSH and chosen comment, path, and passphrase.
primary_path.output: Public and private SSH key files.
primary_path.success_signal: ssh-keygen confirms where the files were stored.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: A key-generation, filesystem, or policy error stops setup.

primary_path.action: Add the private SSH key to ssh-agent.
details: For ED25519, the guide shows ssh-add ~/.ssh/id_ed25519.
primary_path.success_signal: ssh-add completes for the key.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: Agent availability, key path, or passphrase issues must be resolved.

primary_path.action: Copy the public SSH key contents.
details: Use the documented operating-system command or copy the complete public key manually.
Do not copy the private key.
primary_path.output: Public key text on the clipboard.
primary_path.success_signal: The complete public key is available to paste.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: Copying the wrong or incomplete key prevents correct setup and can expose a secret if the private key is copied.

primary_path.action: Open the GitLab SSH keys settings.
details: Sign in, select the avatar, select Edit profile, then Access > SSH keys.
Select Add new key.
primary_path.success_signal: The Key and Title fields are visible.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: Instance permissions or account access can block the settings page.

primary_path.action: Enter the SSH key properties.
details: Paste the complete public key into Key.
Enter a descriptive Title.
Optionally choose Usage type and update Expiration date; Authentication & Signing is the default usage.
primary_path.success_signal: Required key and title values are present.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: GitLab rejects compromised keys and requires a new pair.

primary_path.action: Select Add key.
details: GitLab stores the public key on the account.
primary_path.success_signal: The new key appears in the account's SSH key list.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: Invalid, compromised, duplicate, or policy-restricted keys can be rejected.

primary_path.action: Verify the SSH connection to the correct GitLab host.
details: Identify the official host-key fingerprint for the instance.
Run ssh -T git@gitlab.com or the documented self-managed equivalent.
On the first connection, compare the fingerprint and follow the authenticity prompt.
primary_path.input: Registered key and correct instance host.
primary_path.success_signal: The terminal prints Welcome to GitLab, <username>!
primary_path.failure_or_wait: A missing welcome message requires SSH troubleshooting.

primary_path.action: Open Create new and select New project/repository.
details: This begins the first-commit tutorial's sample project.
primary_path.success_signal: Create blank project is available.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: Project-creation permission can be absent in an organization namespace.

primary_path.action: Choose the blank-project route and name the project My sample project.
details: GitLab generates the project slug from the name.
primary_path.success_signal: The project form shows the name and slug.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: Naming rules or namespace collisions can require a different slug.

primary_path.action: Ensure Initialize repository with a README is selected.
details: The tutorial leaves other project fields to the developer.
The general project form can also contain visibility, optional deployment target, SAST, and secret-detection choices.
primary_path.output: Project configured with an initial README.
primary_path.success_signal: README initialization is selected.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: Visibility or organizational controls can constrain the remaining fields.

primary_path.action: Select Create project.
details: GitLab creates the project and initial repository.
primary_path.output: Sample GitLab project with README and default branch.
primary_path.success_signal: The project overview opens with the initialized repository.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: Quota, permission, naming, or instance errors can prevent creation.

primary_path.action: On the project overview, select Code and copy the Clone with SSH URL.
details: The URL identifies the newly created project repository.
primary_path.input: Created project and registered SSH key.
primary_path.success_signal: The clone URL is copied.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: Choosing HTTPS would require a different authentication route.

primary_path.action: Open a terminal and move to the local directory where the repository should be cloned.
details: The directory must be writable.
primary_path.success_signal: The current working directory is ready.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: Filesystem permission or path issues can block cloning.

primary_path.action: Clone the repository with git clone and the copied SSH URL.
details: The tutorial example is git clone git@gitlab.com:gitlab-example/my-sample-project.git.
primary_path.input: SSH clone URL and working SSH authentication.
primary_path.success_signal: Git reports a successful clone.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: SSH, network, hostname, permission, or existing-directory errors can fail the clone.

primary_path.action: Change into the cloned repository directory.
details: Run cd my-sample-project.
primary_path.success_signal: Git commands operate on the sample repository.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: A different generated path requires using that actual directory name.

primary_path.action: Run git branch and identify the default branch.
details: The current branch is marked with an asterisk.
Press Q if the output opens in a pager.
primary_path.success_signal: The default branch is displayed.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: Instance defaults can differ from main.

primary_path.action: Create and switch to example-tutorial-branch.
details: Run git checkout -b example-tutorial-branch.
primary_path.input: Local repository on the default branch.
primary_path.success_signal: Git reports the new branch and it becomes current.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: An existing branch of the same name or repository error requires resolution.

primary_path.action: Open README.md in a text editor and add the tutorial text.
details: Add Hello world! I'm using Git! and save the file.
primary_path.success_signal: The file contains the new text.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: An unsaved edit will not appear to Git.

primary_path.action: Run git status and confirm README.md is modified but not staged.
details: The expected output names README.md under changes not staged for commit.
primary_path.success_signal: README.md is listed as modified.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: If no change appears, verify the file and repository path.

primary_path.action: Stage README.md.
details: Run git add README.md.
primary_path.success_signal: git add completes.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: A wrong path or filesystem error prevents staging.

primary_path.action: Run git status again and confirm README.md is staged.
details: The expected output lists README.md under changes to be committed and may show the filename in green.
primary_path.success_signal: README.md is listed under changes to be committed.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: If it remains unstaged, repeat or troubleshoot the add command.

primary_path.action: Commit the staged change.
details: Run git commit -m "I added text to the README file".
primary_path.input: Staged README.md and configured Git author identity.
primary_path.success_signal: Git prints a new commit summary.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: Missing user.name or user.email can prompt additional Git configuration; hooks or signing policy can also block the commit.

primary_path.action: Push example-tutorial-branch to GitLab.
details: Run git push origin example-tutorial-branch.
The guide says the branch becomes available in GitLab and visible to other project users.
primary_path.input: Local branch with commit and authenticated SSH remote.
primary_path.output: Remote branch and commit in GitLab.
primary_path.success_signal: The push succeeds and the branch is visible in GitLab.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: SSH, permission, protected-branch, network, or remote errors can fail the push.

primary_path.action: Check out the repository's default branch.
details: The tutorial shows git checkout main; use the actual branch identified earlier if different.
primary_path.input: Local repository with pushed topic branch.
primary_path.success_signal: Git reports the default branch as current.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: Uncommitted changes or a different default name can alter the command.

primary_path.action: Merge example-tutorial-branch into the default branch.
details: Run git merge example-tutorial-branch.
The tutorial directly merges locally, while noting that GitLab typically uses a merge request.
primary_path.output: Merged README commit in the local default branch.
primary_path.success_signal: Git reports a successful merge.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: Conflicts or branch divergence require resolution.

primary_path.action: Push the merged default branch.
details: Run git push.
primary_path.success_signal: The push succeeds.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: Protected default branches, permissions, or repository policies can reject a direct push.

primary_path.action: Open the project and verify the README and last commit.
details: Scroll to the README and confirm the new text is visible.
Above README.md, confirm the Last commit column shows the tutorial commit message.
primary_path.success_signal: README.md shows Hello world! I'm using Git! and Last commit shows I added text to the README file.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: If the content or commit is absent, inspect the pushed branch and default-branch merge.

branches.condition: The intended success is the first GitLab CI/CD pipeline.
branches.path: Create or obtain a project and Maintainer or Owner role; on GitLab.com use an instance runner, otherwise open Settings > CI/CD > Runners and verify an active runner with a green circle or install and register GitLab Runner with the shell executor; open Code > Repository; select the branch; choose plus and New file; name it .gitlab-ci.yml; paste the four-job build, test, and deploy sample; select Commit changes; wait for the pipeline; open Build > Pipelines; verify three stages; select the pipeline ID; select a job such as deploy-prod and inspect its status and log. The official terminal message is that the first CI/CD pipeline was successfully created.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Changes the boundary from a visible repository commit to a completed pipeline and adds runner availability, CI configuration, execution wait, and job inspection.

branches.condition: The developer uses a self-managed instance with self-registration enabled.
branches.path: Open the sign-in page, select Register now or navigate to /users/sign_up, submit the instance's registration form, complete any configured email confirmation or administrator approval, then sign in. If self-registration is disabled, an administrator must create or provision the account.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Adds instance-specific registration, verification, approval, SSO, or provisioning gates before the tutorial.

branches.condition: The developer cannot directly push or merge to the default branch.
branches.path: After pushing the topic branch, create a merge request, satisfy any review, approval, CI, and policy requirements, merge through GitLab, then verify README.md and the commit on the default branch.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Adds collaboration and policy steps and may add waits before the same visible result.

branches.condition: The developer chooses Clone with HTTPS instead of SSH.
branches.path: Use the HTTPS clone URL and an authentication method permitted by the instance, such as an appropriate token, instead of completing the SSH-key route.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Replaces SSH setup and host verification with HTTPS credential setup.

friction_gates.description: GitLab offers many getting-started routes without one platform-wide first success.
The tutorial requires the ability to sign in to a GitLab instance.
Self-managed administrators can disable self-registration or require administrator-driven creation or approval.
The first-commit route requires local Git.
The SSH route requires a supported OpenSSH client.
The developer must create and protect an SSH key pair and add the public key to GitLab.
The developer may need to verify the GitLab host fingerprint on the first SSH connection.
Project creation and default-branch write or merge permissions depend on the namespace and project policies.
git commit requires configured author identity even though the tutorial does not separately enumerate that setup.
Protected-branch or merge-request policies can reject the tutorial's direct default-branch push.

sources.title: Get started with GitLab
evidence_supported: GitLab's many peer workflows and unresolved platform-wide boundary

sources.title: Tutorial: Make your first Git commit
sections_used: Create a branch and make changes
evidence_supported: Full representative path and terminal verification

sources.title: Use SSH keys with GitLab
sections_used: Check for existing SSH key pairs
Add an SSH key to your GitLab account
evidence_supported: Complete SSH prerequisite path, success message, and failures

sources.title: Create users
sections_used: Create a user on the sign-in page
Create a user in the Admin area
Create a user with an authentication integration
evidence_supported: Self-managed account routes, administrator handoff, and email setup

sources.title: Tutorial: Create and run your first GitLab CI/CD pipeline
sections_used: View the status of your pipeline and jobs
evidence_supported: Complete first-pipeline alternative and its terminal milestone

uncertainties.question: Which GitLab workflow is the comparison supposed to measure?
uncertainties.impact: A first project, commit, merge request, pipeline, runner, API call, deployment, or security scan produces different step counts and gates.
uncertainties.reason_unresolved: The official getting-started index presents separate peer workflows and the roster supplies no developer intent.

uncertainties.question: Which GitLab offering and account route applies?
uncertainties.impact: GitLab.com, Self-Managed, and Dedicated can differ in registration, email confirmation, SSO, administrator approval, project limits, runners, and policies.
uncertainties.reason_unresolved: The representative tutorial supports an arbitrary GitLab instance and assumes sign-in access.

uncertainties.question: Can the developer directly push the tutorial merge to the default branch?
uncertainties.impact: A protected branch can require a merge request, approvals, and passing CI, adding waits and actors.
uncertainties.reason_unresolved: The tutorial directly merges and pushes for teaching purposes while noting that GitLab typically uses a merge request.

excluded_after_success: Additional commits and branches after the verified README change.
Merge-request collaboration, approvals, and review when the tutorial's direct merge is permitted.
CI/CD pipeline customization, runners, deployments, releases, security scans, infrastructure, monitoring, GitLab Duo, and API integrations beyond the selected first-commit boundary.
